comment_body: For what it's worth, I built a Git merge driver called [weave](<URL> that tackles part of this problem. It uses tree-sitter to parse code and merge at the function/class level instead of lines. This handles the common case where git produces false conflicts because two branches added to the same line range (different functions, same location). It doesn't do LLM-based semantic resolution (yet), but it eliminates most false positives. 31/31 clean merges vs git's 15/31 on concurrent edits. Plugs in as a standard git merge driver, works transparently with / / .

I think a crate makes sense. A "bevy-centric" undo in the classic mode would probably look something like this: * A Resource with an undo stack and redo stack. * The Undo action pops an entry off of the undo stack, consumes it, and produces a redo action, plus side effects. * The inverse for the Redo action. * The actions themselves are maybe something like ECS Commands? It's hard to say what they will need access to. * Each action has a method which returns a text label for the UI. You can look at the top of the undo or redo stack to get the label, or None if the stack is empty. Coalescing is going to be a challenge because of type erasure. In an app where the possible undoable actions are a closed set, you can represent undo actions with an enum and then just to a match. But because we want the editor to be open-ended, we can't model the actions with an enum; so this means that undo actions need to be downcastable in order to determine whether the top-of-stack action can be merged with the next action. For the nested modality question, I'd do the easiest possible thing, which is to have two resources, each with their own undo and redo stack: AppUndo and TextUndo. TextUndo handles ctrl-z while a text input is focused, and can be used standalone even in apps that don't have AppUndo support. I said earlier that I didn't want to have separate undo frameworks for text input and for the bevy editor; but that statement only is true if the undo framework is non-trivial. If it's tiny and simple, then I don't care if there are two of them. Having an undo that just deals with text strings, and which is built in to text input, might yield significant complexity savings for devs who don't need a complex undo system.

Actually, we think we succeeded in implementing a solution that is based on modifying alone, i.e. which does not require any breaking changes, neither with the encoders/decoders nor the callers that read or write images. Our solution addresses the following major issues: 1. Open the file only once, i.e. avoid a dedicated call only to resolve the decoder. 2. Avoid redundancies / code duplicates between and functions. 3. Add full unicode support by using , which requires C++ 17 support (if C++ 17 is not available, unicode support remains platform-specific). Before creating a PR, we would like to discuss the changs first because a line-by-line review is not really helpful. Our main changes can be summarized as: 1. As now depends on , we moved the deocode/encode functions up such that they are callable by . 2. Adding a function that resolves the path, including unicode support if C++ 17 is available. 3. Adding a function that reads the whole file content, including resolving of unicode paths using . 4. Now, basically only reads the file content and calls therefter. Additionally, the filename is forwarded to because there could be decoders that require explicit writing of a temporary file, still currently this only holds for the gdcm decoder (as discussed above). As long as there is no additional stream API for the decoders, it is more efficient to pass the filename to the decoder instead of writing the file content into a temporary file. The latter is only used if not passed by the caller, e.g. encoding from external buffers, keeping the current behavior. 5. Slightly optimize the decoding process: First, cache the maximum signature length inside to only query this once from all decoders. Second, add the most commonly used decoders (PNG, JPG, TIFF) first such that in most cases, one of the first queries is correct. 6. takes an OutputArray instead of a Mat and includes the type check previously implemented in . Alternatively, is only allowed to take a Mat but not an Outpu

Hi <USER> — to clarify the scope here: Mermaid itself is a rendering library that produces a single SVG. Pan/zoom is a viewer-level concern, and that's where it has actually landed: - GitHub ships built-in pan/zoom for Mermaid blocks - Mermaid Live Editor and Mermaid Chart both have zoom controls - Obsidian, VS Code preview, and several others provide their own zoom UI on top of the rendered SVG For self-hosted pages where you control the markup, the standard recipe is to disable the width cap so the SVG renders at its natural size and is scrollable in its container: or per-diagram: If you want true pan/zoom (mouse wheel, drag, pinch), wrap the rendered SVG with a small library — svg-pan-zoom or <USER>'s svg-toolbelt (mentioned upthread) both work well with Mermaid output. So there isn't a "patch release" planned for this in core — it's intentionally left to the host. If you're hitting this in a specific environment (GitLab, a particular docs site, etc.), happy to look at that case specifically.

The original problem was that IPv6 was useless on production servers, due to the fact that there was no supported way to get a predictable, routable, DNSable IPv6 address. ## The dilemma I think that for this issue and for #362 the question is: **Should behaves as "general" or "git-style" tool ?** What I mean by this is summarized in the following table | | general tool | git style tool | |:--------------|:-------------:|:-----------------------------------:| | examples | , | , | | ignore files | no | yes | | configuration | flags only | flags, files, environment variables | Actually is in between the two worlds. And respecting the ignore files without the configuration feature is bad, IMO. So should choose the red pill or the blue pill ;) ## My proposal Or may be we don't have to choose and we can have both. I think that : - should be a **general tool** (by default) - there should be a flag that makes it act in a "git style" - should be another compiled version from the same code but with different default behavior, that is equivalent to and heaving flag to switch it to "general tool". My arguments for creating the additional are the following: - it should be easy to make the automation tool to build two versions, one with option as default, the other with as default. - the alias solution discussed already in #362 is very "shell specific" : - there is no general alias that can work in all shells ( and included) - every time we download we should setup the aliases, so the advantage of "no install, just download and use it" is not valid any more - aliases are not working when you wan to make a general script (let's say in python) using - this solve the backward compatibility with ignore files as any user can decide simply to switch to or even to rename fo and continue using it as before (respecting the ignore files by default) - this allows to solve not only this issue but also #362 and all other issues that ask to change the default behavior of (this would be done using the configuration file in ) - having both versions will make the supporters on both sides ("pro-general" and the "pro-git") happy

> How could those headers help with partial write handling if it is unclear from them which samples were written? If I am sending series A, B, C, and B & C were written successfully, I'd like the headers to tell me that so that I could only retry sending A. At this point in time, the headers will only tell me that 2 series were written successfully out of 3. Could you explain that a little bit? Yes and great question <USER>! They help without that low level information. We started explaining why in our [proposal](<URL> but it could be expanded, I will make note to extend this point. TL;DR: It's already a huge improvement to know and decide what you want to do with the knowledge IF you are hitting partial write and in what capacity (how many samples) -- on both caller and receiver. It's not trivial (complexity, efficiency) to now communicate back what exactly was not sent and why. Imagine now doubling the network cost and writing/receiving on failures, risking cascading failures. Instead we propose that servers, on partial response, ask for retry with all samples, on fails with the partial "retryable" errors. The server needs to be idempotent as [described](<URL> even if it's a duplication failure for other samples in the batch (it's acceptable, but one could consider making duplicate sample a 200 e.g. in Thanos). Does this help?
